title: Millionaires ask Andy Burnham to tax them more
description: Millionaires including former footballer Gary Lineker and music producer Brian Eno have written to new prime minister Andy Burnham asking to be taxed
body: Millionaires including former footballer Gary Lineker and music producer Brian Eno have written to new prime minister Andy Burnham asking to be taxed more.In an open letter, 120 well-off Britons told Burnham: "We can afford it. We're not talking about higher taxes on those who get up and go to work for their income every day, but on the very richest whose income is derived from the wealth they hold."Organised by Patriotic Millionaires, the letter said it would lead to a more equal society, and urged a "devolution of wealth and power from the very richest". People can already give money or stocks voluntarily to the Treasury using its donation facility.Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch said Lineker is "very welcome to pay more tax he can write a cheque to the treasury, no one is stopping him."The group supports a 2% tax on wealth over £10m."Millionaires are a patriotic bunch," the letter states. "We love this country and we want it to succeed."But success requires investment and a primary source of untouched capital investment is sitting with us, in untaxed potential."Other signatories include Richard Curtis, director of Notting Hill and Love Actually and Ian Gregg, the former managing director of Greggs and son of the bakery chain's founder.The renewed call for higher tax on the wealthy follows a similar campaign in previous years. Burnham did not rule out a wealth tax when asked about it by Lineker a few days before he became prime minister.He suggested he may have "to ask for a little more" tax at some point.The latest call from Patriotic Millionaires said there is a "need to embrace a new kind of devolution of wealth and power, from the very richest in order to reinvest back into our greatest asset in every region".It added that in its own poll, the majority of millionaires wanted a higher tax on themselves. "There are a few people left with outdated economic thinking and few others desperate to hold onto every penny they can... Those that can't see past the end of their own self interest have no place in designing a Britain for the future," the letter said.

schema.headline: Millionaires ask Andy Burnham to tax them more

title: Multiple sclerosis: First drug to help patients walk now on NHS in England
description: The first drug to help people with multiple sclerosis walk is being made available on the NHS in England.   Fampridine boosts nerve signals to make it
body: The first drug to help people with multiple sclerosis walk is being made available on the NHS in England.   Fampridine boosts nerve signals to make it easier for some people to move and live independently. Aysen Slack, who used to pay for fampridine privately until it became too costly, said the decision would make a "huge difference".Up to 5,000 people in England could benefit each year. The drug is already available elsewhere in the UK. Multiple sclerosis is caused by the immune system attacking the protective covering around nerve cells - known as myelin. This damage impairs the ability of nerves to carry electrical messages from the brain to the rest of the body - including those needed for the precise control of muscles needed to walk.Fampridine is known as a "signal booster" as it helps those electrical messages still get through. Clinical trials showed around four in 10 people taking the drug had improvements in their walking speed and in how long they could walk for. This could be the difference between getting around the house or needing help at home or enabling people to go out and see friends and family. Current NHS support includes physiotherapy and walking aids. The drug has previously been rejected for use by the NHS in England as it was not considered value for money, although it did become available in Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland. Aysen Slack, 65 and from Eastbourne, chose to pay privately for the pills once she heard about them. She said fampridine was "working well for me" but came at a "significant expense and I could not keep doing that forever" so she had to stop. Slack said it was "great news" the drug was coming to the NHS and she "would definitely like to try" again. "My mobility has decreased a lot and even in my flat I have to use sticks now," she said, "it would make a huge difference to my life if I were able to improve my walking."More than 120,000 people live with MS in England. However, only around 5,000 people a year will be eligible - based on the extent of their walking difficulties. Patients will try the medicine for up to a month and only those showing "clear benefit" will be able to continue taking it. Prof Frankie Swords, the NHS national medical director, said: "Walking difficulties can have a huge impact on the freedom and independence of people with MS, so this signal-boosting pill could be life-changing for thousands of patients."Even a modest improvement could mean getting around the house more easily, going out with greater confidence and living more independently."Ceri Smith, from the MS Society, said they were "delighted" by the news as the drug could be "life-changing" for many, allowing people to "live more independently or stay in employment".

schema.headline: Multiple sclerosis: First drug to help patients walk now on NHS in England

title: Cwmbran mum run over by own car while saving cat, inquest hears
description: An animal lover was run over and killed by her own car after she got out to save a cat as the handbrake failed on a slope, an inquest has heard.Jacque
body: An animal lover was run over and killed by her own car after she got out to save a cat as the handbrake failed on a slope, an inquest has heard.Jacqueline Davies, 75, climbed out of the driving seat of her red Ford Fiesta to move the cat to safety on 6 July while on a hill, but her car rolled forward and fatally struck her.Gwent Coroner's Court heard Davies, from Hollybush in Cwmbran, Torfaen, was able to dial 999 after the incident, but died in hospital.In a statement, Davies' family said she had a "strength that inspired those around her" and added they were "desperate for answers".The inquest heard Davies was taken to University Hospital of Wales in Cardiff with serious injuries, and died the following day.Coroner Rose Farmer said her provisional cause of death was given as a major haemorrhage, injury to the inferior vena cava - the largest vein in the body - and trauma.Her family said: "We are heartbroken beyond words at the sudden loss of our beloved mum."She was a strong, independent, spirited mum who was never scared to speak her mind, and that was one of the many things we loved about her."She was honest, determined and full of character, with a strength that inspired those around her."Our family is devastated and life will never be the same without her."We are trying to come to terms with what has happened but we are also desperate for answers."Gwent Police wants anyone travelling in the area of Bryn Millwr, Hollybush, on 6 July between 10:00 BST and 10:30 who may have witnessed the crash to contact officers.The inquest into Davies' death will resume on 13 April 2027.

schema.headline: Cwmbran mum run over by own car while saving cat, inquest hears

title: Is 2026 the Commonwealth Games' last dance or a future-proofing revamp?
description: The 2026 Commonwealth Games were thrown into doubt when its original hosts, the Australian state of Victoria, pulled out in 2023 citing spiraling cost
body: The 2026 Commonwealth Games were thrown into doubt when its original hosts, the Australian state of Victoria, pulled out in 2023 citing spiraling costs. That decision took even the leaders of Commonwealth Sport by surprise.After more than a year of uncertainty, Glasgow stepped in to host the Games for the second time, following on from the highly successful event in the Scottish city in 2014.But, with limited preparation time a significant factor, the Games' return to Glasgow a much smaller event.There was competition across 18 sports in 2014 – a number that rose to 20 for Birmingham 2022 - but only 10 sports will feature this year.There are expected to be around 2,000 fewer athletes and no new stadiums have been built, with the athletics events having moved from the 46,000-seater Hampden Park to the Scotstoun Stadium, which will hold just over 10,000 people.Costs of running the event have been cut from between £700m and £800m for Birmingham 2022 and the Gold Coast Games in 2018 to £160m for Glasgow.Whether by accident or design, organisers insist the situation has created a template for the future.They believe a smaller, cheaper, more sustainable Commonwealth Games can last."I don't think we have ever been stronger," Katie Sadleir, chief executive of Commonwealth Sport, told BBC Sport."We took a hard look at ourselves and realised we needed to reset and reimagine this Games so it is an attractive proposition for our members."Crucially, the Commonwealth Games have already secured the Indian city of Ahmedabad as host for 2030, when the number of sports is expected rise to between 15 and 18.Several countries have also expressed interest in hosting in 2034. New Zealand has done so publicly while Nigeria remains interested after challenging Ahmedabad's bid for 2030.Having begun in 1930 as the British Empire Games, the Commonwealth Games face questions about their place and relevance in the modern world.England weightlifter Emily Campbell told the BBC the Games are "one of the best things that came out of colonisation" while three-time Olympic swimming gold medallist Tom Dean insists they can be a "force for good" despite the difficult past."It is even more reason to push the Commonwealths into the spotlight because it shows the best side of sport," he told BBC Sport.However, the Glasgow edition has been hit by the absence of star names.Injuries, such as those to Olympic gold-medal winning gymnast Max Whitlock and Australia's Olympic backstroke champion Kaylee McKeown, are unavoidable and unfortunate. But other athletes have opted to prioritise different events.Olympic 800m champion Keely Hodgkinson is skipping Glasgow in favour of the European Athletics Championships, which start in Birmingham on 10 August and are arguably the bigger track and field event this summer.Dina Asher-Smith, the fastest British woman in history, and Olympic 100m champion Julien Alfred of St Lucia have also pulled out. Canadian swimming superstar Summer McIntosh is another notable absentee.But it is not all doom and gloom.Josh Kerr, fresh from breaking the mile world record, will return to home soil to race for Scotland, as will McColgan.Katerina Johnson-Thompson is chasing her third Commonwealth heptathlon title in a row and Adam Ramsay-Peaty returns to the same pool in which he won two golds in 2014.The Commonwealth Games remain a pinnacle for sports like netball and there is also an increased focus on Para-sport, with this the most integrated Games ever.Six of the 10 sports include Para-competitions with a Commonwealth Games record 47 medal events. Golds, silvers and bronzes won in Para-events will, unlike with other multi-sport events, count towards the overall medal total."There are not many opportunities for the integration of multi-sports [events]," Paralympic swimming champion Brock Whiston told BBC Sport."Without the Commonwealth Games, the smaller countries don't get the chance to show how powerful that integration is."The Commonwealth Games is vital to ensuring there are equal opportunities."English swimmer James Guy, another Olympic champion who will be at his fourth Commonwealth Games in Glasgow, told BBC Sport the event remains a crucial part of an athlete's development."It is a bit easier to make than the GB team," he said."It is a really good way to get on there, get on a relay and get a medal and that is really important for development so the younger guys and girls can progress."As ever, another major factor in assessing the potential longevity of the Commonwealth Games will be the response from fans.Around 1.3 million tickets were sold in 2014 – a figure that will certainly not be matched this time.Sales have been strongest for athletics and cycling but tickets remain available for all sports across the 11 days of competition.There is also a significant change in the UK this year with the BBC, who had provided coverage for 18 Commonwealth Games in a row, being replaced by TNT Sports as the live television broadcaster. The Games are also available to watch on streaming service HBO Max.While radio coverage remains on BBC Radio 5 Live, it means there will be no free-to-air live TV coverage.That makes cutting through to the general public more challenging at a crucial time.The next 11 days remain significant for the Games' future.

schema.headline: Is 2026 the Commonwealth Games' last dance or a future-proofing revamp?

title: Thousands evacuated in France fires as firefighter killed in Italy
description: Around 12,000 people have been evacuated from the south-west of France as the country battles ongoing wildfires days after two firefighters died near
body: Around 12,000 people have been evacuated from the south-west of France as the country battles ongoing wildfires days after two firefighters died near Bordeaux airport, French media reported.The Gironde region government said more than 2,000 hectares (4,000 acres) of land have been destroyed. The use of internal combustion and electric engines have been banned in predominantly forested areas, and a number of roads have been closed. No injuries have been reported on Thursday.Meanwhile in Italy, authorities have paid tribute to a firefighter who died tackling a wildfire in San Cataldo, as Sicily President Renato Schifani wished a "speedy recovery" to another involved in the same operation.Italian Interior Minister Matteo Piantedosi paid tribute to late firefighter Alessandro Marchi who he said died "in service to the community"."He will not be forgotten," he said in a post on X.In an update on Wednesday, head of the Sicilian Civil Protection Salvo Cocina said there had been more than 350 fires on the island including ten major ones which led to precautionary evacuations.He said the fires had human causes - adding that firefighting has become a social and public order problem.Meanwhile, in France, the AFP news agency reported that 10,000 tourists were among those evacuated.France has been paying tribute to the two firefighters who died earlier in the week. Interior Minister Laurent Nuñez posted photos from an event that took place on Wednesday - the day after their deaths were announced.He said the moment was filled with "immense emotion" as he expressed "infinite gratitude" and "unwavering support" to their families.The Gironde region remains under a red alert, with some leisure sites in exposed forest areas to be closed on Thursday afternoon and evening.Yassmina de Freitas, 19, who lives in the coastal town of Le Porge, said she noticed smoke and the smell of burning at lunchtime on Wednesday.By the afternoon "ash… [and] half-burned tree bark" were falling, the sky was "very dark" and the smoke was getting "thicker and thicker".At around 20:00 local time (19:00 BST), her family was evacuated by law enforcement and taken to a gymnasium in a neighbouring town. They had to leave two of their cats and four chickens behind as they could not be found in the flurry to leave.She said she could not sleep at all and was waking up every hour to check the fire's progress."I could see the number of hectares burned rising steadily," she said.Further evacuations took place on Thursday morning on the Lège-Cap-Ferret peninsula, south of Le Porge.In the Var region in south-east France, evacuated residents were told they could go home to assess potential damage to properties on Thursday morning. However, local authorities said they should be prepared to evacuate again if needed.Areas in this region are under a "very high" warning for forest fires from Forecaster Météo-France.Several French departments have recently been ravaged by wildfires during a series of unusual and prolonged heatwaves that have swept across Europe this summer.Madrid has also been impacted by fires, but overnight most evacuated residents were told they can return to their homes - with the exception of one area in Villa del Prado.A fire nearby is still burning, and almost 90 personnel and a helicopter have been deployed to get the flames under control.Emergency services said they had carried out a "direct attack" on the fire and shared a video on social media showing emergency service workers approaching the flames with hoses.Local authorities continued to warn of an "extreme risk of fires" on Thursday morning.Spain's weather service AEMET said temperatures of up to 36C are forecast on Thursday afternoon.Countries across Europe have seen record temperatures in recent months. A report released in April by the Copernicus Climate Change Service found at least 95% of Europe saw "above average" annual temperatures in 2025 and identified Europe as the fastest-warming continent.Wildfire-burnt area and fire emissions both reached record levels last year, the report said.

schema.headline: Thousands evacuated in France fires as firefighter killed in Italy

title: US signs landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia
description: The US and Saudi Arabia have signed a landmark deal that will allow the Gulf kingdom to develop a civilian nuclear programme.The deal is described as
body: The US and Saudi Arabia have signed a landmark deal that will allow the Gulf kingdom to develop a civilian nuclear programme.The deal is described as a "peaceful nuclear co-operation agreement" that will provide "great access for American companies in the Saudi nuclear energy program" by the US energy department.Text from the deal has not been made public, but US media report that it could allow Saudi Arabia to enrich uranium in the future - which can be used as fuel for power plants, but also to make nuclear bombs.Experts say that would mark a first for the US and could run the risk of future nuclear proliferation in an already unstable region.The US Department of Energy said a "bilateral safeguards agreement" had been signed along with the co-operation deal. "Together, these two agreements lay the legal foundation for a decades-long, multi-billion-dollar partnership that advances several priority economic and strategic objectives, including nuclear nonproliferation," the statement said. The Saudi government also released a statement saying the deal "represents an extension of existing co-operation between the two countries in the energy sector", noting that it comes after the Saudi leader visited Washington last November.The nuclear issue has been at the centre of the months-long US-Israeli conflict with Iran. The two nations say one of the reasons they attacked Iran in February was to prevent the country from ever developing a nuclear weapon - something that Tehran denies planning to do.Saudi Arabia's de facto ruler, Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, told CBS News in 2018 that his own nation was not planning to acquire a nuclear weapon, but that "without a doubt if Iran developed a nuclear bomb, we will follow suit as soon as possible".Wednesday's announcement came as fighting escalates between the US and Iran. Saudi Arabia, a longtime US ally, hosts several US bases that have been targeted by Iranian attacks. It also comes two days after Iran's allies in Yemen, the Houthis, announced a "maritime blockade" against the kingdom. Specific details of the nuclear deal have not been given but earlier media reports , externalsuggested that, after undertaking a joint study with the US, Saudi Arabia may be allowed to enrich uranium on its soil to produce fuel at a US-built enrichment facility, instead of relying on imported fuel.This could be worth billions of dollars to the US, whose companies could be involved in building a facility to produce enriched uranium, which can be used as fuel for power plants but also to make nuclear bombs."Rest assured, these agreements uphold the highest standards of nuclear safety and nonproliferation, while relying on the world's best nuclear technology and scientists, designed right here in the United States," US Secretary of Energy Chris Wright said. The energy department said the deal would advance both "US and regional security", and would strengthen America's "competitive edge in civil nuclear technology".Nuclear experts and former officials in Israel - the US's top ally in the Middle East - have voiced concern that the plan could ultimately lead to Saudi Arabia being able to develop a nuclear weapon - though other experts say that path would likely take decades and spark pushback from the international community. Israeli Economy Minister Nir Barkat said his country "can manage it" if Saudi Arabia had nuclear power for peaceful means.Israel itself is widely believed to have nuclear weapons, but it neither confirms nor denies that this is the case.Rosemary Kelanic, the director of the Middle East programme at the US-based Defense Priorities think tank, said it would be "quite shocking" if the US were to allow Saudi Arabia uranium enrichment facilities."The US has never done that before. We have never helped another country enrich on their own soil," she said, adding that this was because "if you can produce your own nuclear fuel, you become a much bigger risk" for building nuclear weapons.The agreement will now be sent to the US Congress for review.  Some lawmakers on both sides of the political aisle are expected to oppose the deal, though Trump's Republican Party controls both the upper and lower chambers and opponents of the deal do not appear to have the votes needed to block it.US Secretary of State Marco Rubio rebuffed concerns that it could advance Saudi Arabia's ability to develop nuclear arms."The US is not going to reach any agreement with any country in the world that leads to the risk of proliferation," Rubio told reporters.Several Democrats opposed to the deal pointed out that, during his time as a senator, Rubio had endorsed a law - the "No Nuclear Weapons for Saudi Arabia Act" - which required Congressional approval of any nuclear deal. Massachusetts Democratic Senator Edward Markey condemned Rubio and Trump as "allowing Saudi Arabia, a belligerent and authoritarian nation, to develop nuclear weapons technologies while starting a war with Iran under the guise of preventing an Iranian nuclear bomb", adding that their "hypocrisy is matched only by their recklessness".If an enrichment provision is included, it would distinguish this agreement from another one struck by the US with the United Arab Emirates in 2009, under which the UAE does not produce its own fuel and accepts a host of other restrictions.For that reason, the agreement is also expected to attract concern from nuclear non-proliferation campaigners, whose ultimate aim is to prevent an increase in the number of nuclear weapons worldwide.But energy sector expert Bob McNally, a former member of US President George W Bush's National Security Council, suggested the US was not creating a "great" security risk. "I think we have a good record of working with countries to responsibly develop their nuclear sectors," he told the BBC.Saudi Arabia has been lobbying for such a deal for years, but Washington had held out on the proviso that it recognises Israel, a key US ally, and normalises diplomatic and economic relations with it. But that demand appears to have now been dropped, according to US media reports. Trump has worked to cultivate a closer relationship with Saudi Arabia during his time in the White House. Along with the strategic and commercial gains likely to come with this deal, one possible reason for this agreement happening now is that Russia and China have reportedly been in discussions to strike a similar deal and the US might not have wanted to miss out.The deal marks a major victory for Saudi Arabia, and it has the potential to reshape power dynamics in the Middle East - though not in the immediate future.

schema.headline: US signs landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia

title: Houthis claim attack on Saudi tankers in Red Sea
description: The Houthis named the tankers as the Encelia and the Layla, though their responsibility has not been confirmed.The group's military spokesperson Yahya
body: The Houthis named the tankers as the Encelia and the Layla, though their responsibility has not been confirmed.The group's military spokesperson Yahya Saree said they had targeted the ships with drones and missiles for allegedly violating its naval blockade. Saree added that the Houthis promised to continue their naval operations and would continue "enforcing the 'siege for a siege' equation". The state-run Saudi Press Agency confirmed early on Thursday that the Encelia was struck "while sailing in the Red Sea", resulting in a fire on its bow. Citing an unnamed official source at the Transport General Authority, it described the attack as a violation of international law. All crew were safe, it reported.UK Maritime Trade Operation (UKMTO) reported an "unknown projectile" struck a Saudi tanker about 130km from Al Shuqaiq in Saudi Arabia at 20:00 GMT. The attack caused a fire but no casualties, it said.British maritime risk management group Vanguard also reported that the Saudi tanker Encelia had been hit. The attack on the Layla has not been confirmed. US Central Command (Centcom) said at 21:30 GMT it had launched strikes at Trump's direction "to further degrade Iran's ability to threaten civilian mariners and commercial vessels transiting regional waters".The strikes targeted "Iranian military targets including maritime capabilities, missile and drone storage facilities, coastal surveillance sites, and air defense assets," it said.Kuwait, Jordan and Bahrain soon reported responding to airborne threats, before Iran's military said they had targeted US assets in all three countries, according to AFP.Iranian state media reported two people had been killed in US strikes on the country's south near its border with Iraq. Meanwhile, the Iranian Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) said the Strait of Hormuz was "completely closed" after an explosion in the channel, Iranian state media reported. The IRGC said it had stopped three oil tankers from passing through the strait.One tanker caught fire and two others turned back after attempting to pass through a mined route south of the strait, according to Iranian state media.It comes after Trump promised earlier on Wednesday to attack an Iranian bridge or power plant if its forces hit ships in the Strait of Hormuz. Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi later warned of an "eye for an eye" response to attacks on Iran.On Tuesday, Trump told reporters he would "take care of the Houthis" should they obstruct the Red Sea as they had said. Few details of the "maritime embargo" were given by the Houthis when it was announced on Monday, but a media official said they were closing the Bab al-Mandab Strait - the Red Sea's southern gateway - to Saudi vessels.The Red Sea has become crucial for oil shipments from Saudi Arabia during the US-Israeli war with Iran, which has led to the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz in the Gulf.Now, the threat from the Houthis risks further disruption to international shipping, with hostilities in the region having already led to global fuel prices fluctuating significantly.So far, at least seven oil tankers have made U-turns close to Yemen since the embargo. Saree claimed the Houthis had "forced approximately ten ships to retreat and return".The disruption in the Red Sea comes as peace negotiations have effectively stalled between the US and Iran. Both countries signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) in June to halt military operations and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, as well as to reach an agreement to end the war over the following 60 days.But three weeks after it was signed, Trump declared the ceasefire "over" after Iranian attacks on ships in Hormuz, and US strikes in response.

schema.headline: Houthis claim attack on Saudi tankers in Red Sea

title: Airport drop-off fees up by a third - here are the priciest
description: Drop-off fees at the UK's major airports have risen by a third on average since last summer, new analysis has found.The RAC motoring group looked at t
body: Drop-off fees at the UK's major airports have risen by a third on average since last summer, new analysis has found.The RAC motoring group looked at the cost of pulling up to the closest point to terminals at the 20 busiest airports, 16 of which had put up their prices since last year.London airport Gatwick topped the list by charging a "farewell fee" of £10 for 10 minutes. after lifting it by £3 from last year. Stansted also charges £10 but drivers are allowed 15 minutes at its "express set down" outside departures.Airports UK, the trade body, said that free drop-offs were available and fees were part of airports' business models and climate change objectives.The RAC said that the price of dropping people off in "kiss-and-fly" areas rose by an average of £1.70 to £7 compared to this time last year.  London City Airport, which only started making people pay this year, is the most expensive on a per-minute basis. Its £8 charge for five minutes works out at £1.60 per minute.Birmingham is the cheapest, charging £7.00 for 15 minutes which equates to 47p per minute.  In contrast, none of the top 10 airports in the EU charge for drop-offs, according to RAC research.The RAC acknowledged UK airports generally offered other options for setting people down at locations further from the terminal, sometimes with free shuttle buses.But the motoring organisation's senior policy officer, Rod Dennis said "having a captive audience should be no excuse for these 'farewell fees' – especially when they're completely at odds with how major airports around the world operate". He said drivers needed to be especially careful of barrierless "park now, pay later" drop-off zones, as they could face a parking charge notice if they failed to pay online or by phone within a certain period of time.The Aviation Environment Federation (AEF) campaign group defended the drop-off fees.It said there needs to be an incentive for people to use other forms of transport to reduce congestion and air pollution around airports, which it said can cause a range of childhood illnesses such as asthma.Celeste Hicks, policy manager at AEF, said: "Any parking charges or drop off charges could be reinvested by laying on local bus and rail services instead of lining the pockets of airport shareholders."Airports want more people to arrive by trains and buses.When Transport Secretary Heidi Alexander approved Gatwick's plans for a second runway, one requirement was to have 54% of passengers using public transport.Heathrow may also have to act to try and put people off driving to get a third runway.A London Gatwick spokesperson said drop-off charges were aimed at disincentivising "kiss and fly" trips and data indicated it was "influencing passengers to seek alternative options".The airport links to 120 train stations across London and the south east, with eight local bus routes running 24/7, they said.Passengers can still be dropped off for free at long-stay car parks, with a free shuttle bus to the terminal and Blue Badge holders are exempt from the charge, they said.A spokesperson for Airports UK said where drop off fees are charged, they are "necessary to manage congestion, and the traffic and air pollution concerns of our local communities, as well as our climate change objectives". "These are issues that airports are mandated to manage by the government and local authorities," they said.But Clive Wratten, chief executive of the Business Travel Association, said fees were adding to businesses' costs."First it was environmental [reasons]. Then congestion management. Then funding new routes. Now apparently it is about offsetting tax rises," he said. "The goalposts have moved so many times now that the credibility of any justification has long since gone."

schema.headline: Airport drop-off fees up by a third - here are the priciest

title: Wildberries: Russia's businesses under strain from Ukraine's attacks on retail warehouses
description: Ukrainian drones have hit several retail warehouses belonging to Russia's biggest online retailer, Wildberries, in a matter of days, dealing a blow to
body: Ukrainian drones have hit several retail warehouses belonging to Russia's biggest online retailer, Wildberries, in a matter of days, dealing a blow to a large number of small and medium-sized businesses.After months of drone attacks on oil depots and refineries, Kyiv says it is now targeting logistics hubs used by Russians to buy military equipment, and disrupting supplies to the Russian army.But it has also frustrated hundreds of Russian sellers who depend on online marketplaces for their livelihoods, making the war feel closer to home after almost four and a half years."I am a mother of a disabled child, I've built the business myself," one woman complained on an expletive-laden social media post that went viral."I've lost 1m roubles (£9,500; $12,700)... We're already getting screwed over from every direction as it is."In Ukraine's first attack on Wildberries at the weekend, two warehouses were hit, at Elektrostal east of Moscow and in Kotovsk in the Tambov region, some 400km (250 miles) from the Ukrainian border.Seven workers on a nightshift were killed in Kotovsk and one was fatally wounded at Elektrostal. Dozens more were injured and goods worth an estimated at £750m ($1bn) were destroyed.Kyiv said it had struck "major logistics facilities" used to "supply sanctioned components for drone production and navigation equipment".Unlike Western retail giant Amazon, which sells goods directly alongside its marketplace business, Wildberries and Ozon act almost entirely as marketplaces, connecting independent sellers with buyers across Russia, storing and delivering their goods and handling payments.This means many of the losses incurred in the attacks have been borne by businesses.For one woman, who sells stationery items, Wildberries is the only distribution channel. The day after she delivered her goods to the Elektrostal warehouse near Moscow last Friday, she learned that it had all gone up in flames.She still has other inventory left, but this time of the year is important for her business, as parents and students stock up for the new autumn term.Another Wildberries trader who sells teenage clothing, told the BBC she had lost about 1.4m roubles (£13,300) worth of stock. She is halting new supplies for a few weeks."The outlook for working with Wildberries is very murky," she said. "Right now I have enough of a safety cushion to survive the current losses, but if there are more strikes, I probably won't manage."Neither woman expects compensation. Earlier this month, less than two weeks before the attacks, a clause was added to their contracts, including drone attacks in the list of "force majeure events" for which Wildberries is not liable.Company owner Tatiana Kim has promised "not to abandon" the sellers and some compensation has been paid to a number of small businesses. She has also offered temporary discounts and waived shipment fees, but this has done little to calm fears of further attacks.Supporting small and medium-sized businesses has been among Vladimir Putin's declared priorities even in wartime, and framed as a backbone of resilience in the face of Western sanctions.But the Russian state has been unable to shield the sector from the war's impact, and there have been several shocks to businesses since the start of the year.VAT went up from 20% to 22% in January, with extra funding earmarked for defence, and tax breaks for some businesses were scrapped.Some 209,000 small and medium-sized companies closed in the first quarter of 2026, according to business intelligence platform Kontur.Fokus. That is 9% more than in the first three months of 2025.Widespread internet shutdowns and a crackdown on popular messaging apps led to further troubles, and Moscow businesses lost tens of millions of dollars in one week in March, according to some estimates.And then came the fuel crisis, triggered by Ukrainian strikes on oil depots, refineries and supply routes. "There are already a lot of nails, and they keep hammering them in. Is [the attack on Wildberries warehouses] another nail in the coffin? Well, yes, of course," Prof Ruben Eniklopov of Pompeu Fabra University in Barcelona told the BBC.Small businesses in Russia have proved resilient in the past, but inflation is high, the budget deficit is widening, and oil and gas revenues are 23% lower than in the first six months of last year.Russia has vast financial reserves, accumulated in peacetime, but its war in Ukraine is increasingly swallowing resources, starving the civilian economy of growth.Additional reporting by Olga Shamina.

schema.headline: Wildberries: Russia's businesses under strain from Ukraine's attacks on retail warehouses

title: Police officer was violent rapist who attacked women over 14 years
description: Image source, Police ScotlandImage caption, Detectives have so far uncovered nine victims of PC Alan GreerByScotland investigations correspondentA pol
body: Image source, Police ScotlandImage caption, Detectives have so far uncovered nine victims of PC Alan GreerByScotland investigations correspondentA police officer who took his own life in May was a violent rapist who carried out a spate of sexual attacks and attempted murders of women over at least 14 years, the BBC can reveal.PC Alan Greer's first known victim was a woman he raped in her home in Glasgow in 2012 after she had called the police to report being the victim of a crime.The woman reported the sex attack by Greer late last year, sparking one of Police Scotland's biggest ever anti-corruption inquiries.Detectives have so far uncovered a further eight victims, five of whom were sex workers, attacked while Greer was off duty.It has emerged that Greer used hundreds of prostitutes throughout his police career, and it is anticipated more victims of his violence will be identified.Assistant Chief Constable Lynn Ratcliff, gold commander for the investigation, said what the inquiry had found was "truly shocking".She said: "This officer has clearly abused his power and has revisited someone that he's encountered in the course of his duties."But this victim was brave enough to come and speak to us, and that really was the trigger point for us starting our investigation. "That's allowed us to proactively identify eight further victims."Figure caption, Assistant Chief Constable Lynn Ratcliff said the inquiry into Greer's offending had been "truly shocking"'Violent double life'Greer, who was born in Australia in 1978, moved to the UK when he was six.He joined Strathclyde Police in 2009 before it merged with Police Scotland in 2013 and had 17 years of apparently unblemished service.At first, he worked as a uniformed response officer in Glasgow before he secured a job in officer safety training, based in Coatbridge, in about 2016.This meant he was involved in teaching probationer officers as well as refreshing more experienced officer training.When he died in May, he was 47, with a wife and child.Greer's family and colleagues are understood to have been "oblivious" to his violent double life.Senior officers insisted there were no opportunities to spot his offending sooner.The woman who started the investigationIt was towards the end of last year that a woman in her late thirties walked into a Glasgow police station to report a crime.Somewhat reluctantly, she explained that she had, in 2012, attempted to report a disturbance at her home, and that uniformed officers had attended her Glasgow home.She said the officers took her statement and left.A few hours later one of the officers returned, alone, forced her into a sexual act and raped her.After she told the police last year, the woman seemed to disengage from further inquiries.The case was handed to the anti-corruption unit (ACU), who, by April, had secured a statement from her.From this they were then able to identify the call she'd made in 2012 and could identify the officers who had attended.They found that, in the weeks after the 2012 attack, one of those officers had repeatedly monitored the woman's crime file on the police computer for updates, perhaps to see if she had made a further disclosure about what happened to her later that day.The PC had even carried out one of these checks while on annual leave.Fourteen years later as anti-corruption detectives attempted to identify the woman's attacker, it would be these keystrokes, these searches in her crime file for which there was no legitimate police reason, which gave them their suspect - PC Alan Greer.A timeline of how Alan Greer's crimes came to lightA critical incident was then declared and a gold commander was installed.Anti-corruption detectives started working on Greer, and obtained his mobile phone number, which had previously not been held on his personnel file.Only his home number had been retained by police.The mobile number was then put through police intelligence databases, which revealed a single piece of intelligence dating back to 2018, linked to that number, but with no name attached.The intelligence had come from a Glasgow sex worker and indicated that the owner of that phone number was a customer who had been violent.There was no indication the number belonged to a police officer, but was this an opportunity to catch Greer in 2018?Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Greer joined Strathclyde Police to work as a uniformed response officer in GlasgowACC Ratcliff told the BBC: "I am entirely satisfied that everything that could have been reasonably done at that time was done, and that there were no missed opportunities as a result of that information."The information from 2018 gave detectives a second strand of evidence, and grounds to monitor every keystroke that Greer made on police computers.They then discovered that Greer had been searching the police system for traces of a complaint from a phone number, which detectives were able to identify belonged to a Glasgow sex worker.This was a breakthrough. This was their suspect repeating the pattern from 2012.They now had enough evidence to put before a sheriff who authorised a warrant to search his home and seize his devices.Dawn raid on Greer's homeAt dawn on 24 April, officers raided Greer's family home in what was described as a "day of executive action".Greer appeared shocked to be arrested on suspicion of the 2012 rape.Seven mobile phones and a laptop were seized, and Greer was brought into a police station to be fingerprinted and have his DNA taken.He was not interviewed but was suspended from duty and released pending further enquiries.It was only when officers started to look at his phones that the scale of Greer's offending began to emerge.Thousands of graphic videos and images were recovered, revealing that Greer had used hundreds of prostitutes throughout his police career.Officers are not yet even halfway through the files, but have already identified five sex workers who have been seriously sexually assaulted. If Greer was alive, it is understood police would have sought charges of attempted murder in some of the cases, such was the level of violence used.Three other victims have been identified, including two female police officers Greer had taken inappropriate photographs of while they were training.As Greer's serial offending was emerging, detectives took steps to ensure his family were not in danger, and social work officers, accompanied by local police, visited his home on 5 May.Greer died by suicide the following day.'Police Scotland's Wayne Couzens moment'In the days that followed, police announced Greer's death on the force's internal communications platform, and stated he died while suspended and under criminal investigation.A source close to the investigation told the BBC: "Some of his colleagues thought this was a callous move on behalf of the police, saying he was under criminal investigation when he'd only just died."But it's clear they were all totally in the dark about this guy's offending. "Folk will be in a state of shock when they learn the full truth. I don't think anyone really knows how bad it is yet."It feels a bit like this could be Police Scotland's Wayne Couzens moment."Image source, Met PoliceImage caption, Wayne Couzens murdered Sarah Everard after kidnapping her under the guise of an arrestCouzens is a former Metropolitan Police officer serving a whole-life sentence for the rape and murder of Sarah Everard. He abused his police role to stage a fake arrest, highlighting severe vetting failures and multiple failed opportunities to uncover the risk he posed.ACC Ratcliff said the comparison was unhelpful and that no red flags were missed.She said: "At this stage, we've got no evidence to suggest [missed red flags] is the case, but we absolutely keep that under review because one of the things that we have to do from inquiries of this nature is make sure that we capture and we take on board the lessons learned."The assistant chief constable insisted the vetting landscape in Police Scotland is "markedly different" to when Greer joined Strathclyde Police in 2009, and that Greer would not pass vetting checks today.There are still 1,000s of images recovered from Greer's devices that have to be sifted through, and it is understood he used multiple pseudonyms and phones to carry out his sexual transactions.  Specialist officers have been drafted in from other units to bolster the anti-corruption unit, including from the major incident team, and the inquiry is expected to take many months.Greer will never be brought to justice but police say they are determined to establish the full picture of his offending.ACC Ratcliff said: "If there is anyone who believes that they may have been a victim of Alan Greer, we would urge them to have the courage and confidence to come forward and tell us about it. "They can be assured that we will listen, and that we will investigate, and we will do our best to make sure that they get the support that they need going forward."Production/additional reporting: Kevin Anderson and Carla BasuIf you want to contact Mark about this story, email him at mark.daly@bbc.co.ukFor support and information on the issues raised in this article, visit BBC Action Line.Get our flagship newsletter with all the headlines you need to start the day. Sign up here.

schema.headline: Police officer was violent rapist who attacked women over 14 years

title: Hosepipe ban in force for millions of households - is your area affected?
description: Image source, Matt Cardy/Getty ImagesByMark PoyntingClimate reporterJess CarrData designer and Phil LeakeData journalistUpdated 8 hours agoA hosepipe
body: Image source, Matt Cardy/Getty ImagesByMark PoyntingClimate reporterJess CarrData designer and Phil LeakeData journalistUpdated 8 hours agoA hosepipe ban for Thames Water customers has come into force bringing the number of people placed under a ban to more than 20 million.Such measures have been introduced across large areas of southern and eastern England and parts of Wales because successive heatwaves have left water supplies under strain.But while spring and early summer have been relatively dry for much of the UK - with temperatures regularly exceeding 30C  - winter was much wetter than usual.This means hosepipes cannot be used for watering gardens, washing vehicles and windows and filling pools. It has raised questions about how effectively water resources are being managed, and whether the UK is prepared for drier summers expected with climate change.So how is your area doing and how close are you to a drought?There is no single definition of drought, but it is ultimately caused by a prolonged period of low rainfall, which has knock-on effects for nature, agriculture and water supplies. Environmental bodies in each UK nation decide whether to declare drought or water scarcity based on an assessment of current water levels and long-term weather forecasts.Both of these states can trigger a hosepipe ban. Drought is a lack of precipitation, whereas water scarcity is about a mismatch between water supply and demand - so it is not just about the weather. This summer, bans have been introduced to manage high water demand. Mixed picture across the countryLarge parts of England are currently in "prolonged dry weather" status - the category below drought - according to the Environment Agency. So far, eight UK water companies have announced hosepipe bans, seven in England and one in Wales.Prolonged dry weather has been declared in East Anglia, the West Midlands, Devon, Cornwall and the Isles of Scilly, Lincolnshire and Northamptonshire, Hertfordshire and London, Wessex, Thames, and Solent and the South Downs.The remaining parts of England, mostly in the north, remain in "normal" status.In Wales, areas across the north and south-east have been escalated from normal to prolonged dry weather status, according to Natural Resources Wales, while there are no official droughts currently in Northern Ireland.Scotland does not declare droughts but monitors "water scarcity". Several areas in eastern Scotland are in "moderate scarcity" but none are in the most extreme category of "significant scarcity".Rivers, reservoirs and groundwater - how are supplies?Our three main sources of water are rivers, reservoirs and from deep underground. Following the hot, dry weather, river flows were low in June for parts of the UK."Without significant rainfall we could see increasing impacts on wildlife, agriculture and water resources as the summer progresses," said Steve Turner, a hydrologist at the UK Centre for Ecology & Hydrology (UKCEH).Reservoirs are a crucial part of water supplies and levels in Northern Ireland, Wales and Scotland are all "healthy" and above expected for this time of year. England's reservoirs are below average levels for the time of year, albeit with some variation across the country. South-east England relies more heavily on groundwater than reservoirs.Groundwater originates as rainfall and is naturally stored beneath the surface in the pore spaces and fractures in rocks. In June, groundwater levels were generally at or just below normal, having been topped up by winter rainfall but depleted by a drier spring in most regions.But water can flow more slowly through some rock types than others, sometimes taking years to fully respond to current conditions.These groundwater stores "respond more slowly to changes in the climate than rivers, which is why they provide a useful buffer during periods of drought", said Prof Alan MacDonald of the British Geological Survey.It is why groundwater droughts in the south of England generally take a longer time to develop but can be longer-lasting if they do occur.Is climate change to blame?Droughts are complex phenomena, driven by a mix of natural and human causes.The Met Office expects the UK to experience drier summers on average in future as the world warms, though there has been no clear trend so far.But rising temperatures can play a more direct role by drawing extra moisture from the soil via evaporation."A warmer atmosphere is thirstier for moisture and this can mean water in the soil, rivers and reservoirs are depleted more effectively, leading to more rapidly onsetting droughts, heatwaves and wildfires," said Richard Allan, professor of climate science at the University of Reading.But there are other factors that determine whether dry conditions lead to water shortages, including how we use water.In a landmark review into the water sector of England and Wales, water companies were criticised for historic underinvestment in water infrastructure.As part of plans to address water shortages, the government and water companies are planning nine new reservoirs for England by 2050, in addition to one under construction at Havant Thicket in Hampshire.But the Environment Agency has warned that measures to tackle water leaks and control water demand - potentially including hosepipe bans and more smart meters - may be needed in England too.Water companies in Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland - which are all public bodies or non-profit - also said they were taking steps to secure future supplies.Get in touchTell us how you're affected by the hosepipe ban.

schema.headline: Hosepipe ban in force for millions of households - is your area affected?

title: Africa's camels are dying from extreme heat as temperatures soar
description: Even camels can't cope: Africa's ships of the desert hit by rising temperaturesImage source, ReutersImage caption, Livestock herders in the Horn of Af
body: Even camels can't cope: Africa's ships of the desert hit by rising temperaturesImage source, ReutersImage caption, Livestock herders in the Horn of Africa and North Africa have switched to camels over the years because of their famed resilienceByNavin Singh KhadkaEnvironment correspondentUpdated 00:22 BSTCamels are famously known as the ships of the desert, but even these hardy animals are not being spared from the extremes of rising temperatures in Africa.The Afar region of north-eastern Ethiopia is one of the hottest and driest places on Earth, where nomads often lead camel caravans carrying salt through the desert."I can see my camels suffering from extreme heat," Ali Umer, a camel herder from Semera in Afar, told the BBC."They get blisters on their feet, their eyes become watery and the hot sand burns their skin and eats up their hair when they are sitting," said the 40-year-old who owns around 500 camels."Within the past one month I have lost eight camel calves because of the extreme heat, it has become much more intense and difficult."Even the wind, which used to bring cool air, now carries heat," he said, adding he had noticed these drastic changes in the temperature in recent years.He is not alone in his observations: scientists, animal welfare workers and various studies all mention stress, fatigue, dehydration, disease prevalence as direct impacts of extreme heat.The higher temperatures also indirectly make camels suffer by causing severe loss of water, vegetation, and reduced shaded areas.Image source, Corbis via Getty ImagesImage caption, Camels carry bricks of salt cut from the mineral-laden lakes in the Danakil Depression, where temperatures can top 50CNorth Africa and the Horn of Africa host around 80% of the world's largest population of dromedary camels - those with one hump.Over the years, farmers and cattle herders in these regions have switched to these animals because of their resilience to harsh heat and drought conditions.But a study based on the perception of camel herders in southern Ethiopia, published in 2025, , externalshowed that high temperatures and climate variability had had a detrimental impact on camel herd health."Respondents reported that climate change and variability have affected camels' health, productivity, and reproductive performance," it said.In neighbouring Somalia, the situation appears to be more serious. Research published in June, external has shown that drought-related camel mortality is a widespread crisis, affecting nearly 46% of camel-owning households.The study included nearly 6,000 pastoral households and showed that the mortality was concentrated in northern regions, with Sool reporting a "staggering mortality rate of nearly 73%".Figure caption, Two of Ali Umer's shepherds in Ethiopia's Afar region bathe a one-month-old camel to cool it downExtreme heat is the main cause behind the drought in the region although other factors like inadequate veterinary services and animal management make things worse, experts say.An analysis by atmospheric scientists, external found that high temperatures in the region were behind the 2021-22 drought, as extreme heat makes water from soil and water bodies turn into vapour and plants release water in them into air as vapour.Veterinarians say they are treating more sick camels and are seeing more deaths due to extreme heat and drought."We are seeing significant increase in cases of hyperthermia in camels as a result of which they get multiple infections," Hassan Nuya Guyo, a veterinary officer in Marsabit county in north-eastern Kenya bordering Ethiopia, told the BBC."In the last two years, we have seen a more than 15% increase in camel deaths in our county due to extreme heat."Disease prevalence among camels and reduced milk yield from them are as a result of climate change impacts including heat stress, according to another study conducted in the Horn of Africa, external.Image source, Hassan Nuya GuyoImage caption, Hassan Nuya Guyo, a vet in north-eastern Kenya, says in the last two years there has been a 15% increase in camel deaths in the area he overseesA similar story is playing out in North Africa. A study in Algeria, external, published this year, has shown a significant increase in camel deaths during summer heatwaves. "Time series analysis highlights significant seasonal mortality patterns, underscoring the need for targeted interventions during high-risk periods like peak summer months; such as strategic water provisioning, supplemental feeding, and enhanced disease monitoring," it said.The World Meteorological Organization (WMO) says extreme heat events have become a common phenomenon in several North African countries with heatwaves having increased with a clear upward trend since 1981.It said a number of countries in the region and the Middle East reported temperatures above 50C in 2024.WMO's State of the Climate in Africa 2025 report, external said North Africa recorded the most rapid warming of the six subregions - 0.43C per decade - between 1991 and 2025. Experts say camels comfortably beat the heat up to around 40C by mainly allowing their body temperature to fluctuate by several degrees throughout the day to deal with outside temperature.By doing that they avoid sweating and, combined with concentrated urine and dry faeces, it helps them minimise water loss, while their thick skin coat helps them ward off solar radiation.Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Experts say camels comfortably beat the heat up to around 40C by mainly allowing their body temperature to fluctuate by several degrees throughout the day"But camels can also experience heat stress when temperatures rise beyond their comfort range," said Dr Mohammed Bengoumi, a scientist with the UN's Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), who specialises in camel health and works in North Africa."In many parts of the Sahara, summer temperatures now frequently exceed 50C, increasing the risk of severe heat stress, particularly when accompanied by reduced vegetation cover, water scarcity, and limited access to shade," he told the BBC.Professor Abdelmadjid Chehma at the University of Ghardaia in Algeria, who has conducted experimental works on camels in the Sahara Desert, has a similar conclusion."Our works show that prolonged day-and-night heat above 41 to 45C directly attack their cells,"  he told the BBC."Their immune system suffers because such heat levels damage their white blood cells and other cells because of stress, they [the cells] struggle to repair themselves and end up destroying themselves."For camels this means when heat stress sets in, their metabolism slows down to limit internal heat production leading to sharp reduction in food intake resulting in lethargy and generalised fatigue.That leads to drop in milk production and also causes reproductive disorders.Image source, AFP via Getty ImagesImage caption, Pastoralists who rely on camels for milk are suffering as studies show that heat stress has reduced yieldsProfessor Mohammed el Khasmi, animal hydrobiology expert at the University of Casablanca in Morocco, has a long list of effects of heat stress he and his team have observed on camels.Weight loss, inflammation, muscle and bone related illnesses, chemical imbalance due to kidney malfunction, infectious diseases caused by parasites were a few among them, he said.While extreme heat is the primary culprit, its impacts become severe when other factors like loss of vegetation and water, lack of shaded areas and inadequate veterinary services add up, experts say.This is why many herders in Ethiopia have moved their camels from the northern region, where the animals were found historically, to the south where water and vegetation are relatively available, experts there say."And this is not the seasonal movement of the herders," said Daniel Gelan, professor and pastoralist development researcher with Vision University in Ethiopia.Image source, Ali UmerImage caption, Ali Umer says that within the last month he has lost eight camel calves because of the extreme heat"They are leaving the northern region for good because they can no longer sustain camel herding there because of extreme heat and drought," he told the BBC.The Horn of Africa has been recovering from the longest and most severe recorded drought spanning from 2020 to 2023, but now it is seeing another round of the harsh condition, according to charity organisation Oxfam.It said water prices in some parts of Somalia had risen by more than 2,000% because of the drought.Umer, the cattle herder in Afar, said he was worried that an outbreak of disease could start killing his adult camels anytime now.His concern is valid, according to Prof Chehma."While camels remain the livestock herders' last line of defence against the desert, the unprecedented intensity of current heatwaves directly threatens their vital functions, turning this historically resilient animal into a climate victim," he said.Additional reporting by BBC Amharic's Firehiwot KassaYou may also be interested in:

schema.headline: Africa's camels are dying from extreme heat as temperatures soar

title: Friend of Manchester synagogue attacker jailed over separate plot
description: A friend of the Manchester synagogue attacker has been jailed for life with a minimum of 17 years after they planned a separate attack on a military b
body: A friend of the Manchester synagogue attacker has been jailed for life with a minimum of 17 years after they planned a separate attack on a military base.Mohammad Bashir, 31, drove Jihad Al-Shamie on a 10-hour round trip to conduct hostile reconnaissance at the UK's Defence Academy in Shrivenham, Oxfordshire, on 14 August 2025.Weeks after the trip, Al-Shamie, 35, was shot dead by police after carrying out the Heaton Park Synagogue attack, in which two Jewish men were killed.Bashir was sentenced at Manchester Crown Court after pleading guilty to one count of preparing an act of terrorism.Counter terror police uncovered Bashir's friendship with Al-Shamie after reviewing his phones following the attack on the synagogue in October 2025.This breaking news story is being updated and more details will be published shortly. Please refresh the page for the fullest version.You can receive Breaking News on a smartphone or tablet via the BBC News App. You can also follow @BBCBreaking on X, external to get the latest alerts.

schema.headline: Friend of Manchester synagogue attacker jailed over separate plot

title: Jason Alexander: Seinfeld actor apologises to Courtney Stodden for 'inappropriate' sketch when she was 17
description: US actor Jason Alexander has apologised to reality star Courtney Stodden for an "inappropriate" comedy sketch they appeared in together when she was 1
body: US actor Jason Alexander has apologised to reality star Courtney Stodden for an "inappropriate" comedy sketch they appeared in together when she was 17. The Seinfeld star appeared in a Funny or Die sketch with Stodden in 2012, which parodied daytime talk shows such as Dr Phil. The sketch saw Alexander rubbing a mobile phone on Stodden's breasts because it was the only area with "plenty of signal". After Stodden reposted and questioned the clip earlier this week, Alexander said he was "deeply sorry" and offered her his "sincerest apologies". Writing on Instagram,, external Stodden said: "The older I get, the more impossible this is to understand. "During this comedy sketch, Jason Alexander repeatedly rubbed his phone across my breasts while saying there was 'no signal' in my head but 'plenty of signal' in my chest. "Later in the sketch, he joked that when I turned 18 he'd like to take me behind the couch and 'have his way' with me."Stodden noted that Alexander was credited as one of the writers of the episode, adding: "Looking back now, I don't see comedy. I see a 17-year-old girl whose body became part of an adult joke. "I was a minor. I didn't have the legal power to decide whether I wanted to participate... I was the only child in the room. I felt so alone."Stodden said she chose to speak out after reflecting: "I can't change what happened to me but I can ask myself: How did so many adults look at a minor and decide this was acceptable?"In a statement issued to US publications including Variety, Alexander said: "Looking back at the comedy sketch in which Ms Stodden and I participated in 2012, I completely agree that it was inappropriate and I truly regret it."But more importantly, I am deeply sorry for any harm or distress it has caused Ms Stodden. I offer her my sincerest apologies."Stodden, 31, is currently campaigning for California to pass a bill that would ban child marriage by making the legal minimum age 18 years old. Stodden married 51-year-old actor Doug Hutchison in 2011, when she was 16 years old.The marriage was legal because her parents gave permission. Stodden and Hutchison divorced in 2020.Marrying aged 16 or 17 remains legal in 33 US states with parental consent or a judicial waiver. It was legal in every state until 2018.Stodden told the Hollywood Reporter she "appreciated" Alexander's apology. "People keep asking if he's apologised privately, and he hasn't," she continued. "A private apology would be meaningful, but meaningful action would be even more powerful."If he truly regrets what happened, I'd invite him to support [campaign group] Unchained At Last and the fight to end child marriage through AB 1267. That's how we turn accountability into lasting change."Alexander played George Costanza in Seinfeld and also appeared in ER, Pretty Woman and Shallow Hal. Stodden has appeared on reality shows including Couples Therapy, Celebrity Big Brother, Reality Ex-Wives and Celebs Go Dating.

schema.headline: Jason Alexander: Seinfeld actor apologises to Courtney Stodden for 'inappropriate' sketch when she was 17

title: The BBC News app keeps you informed with live and breaking news you can trust
description: Depending on the contract you have, data charges may apply for accessing the internet on your mobile device.If you are not sure about the potential ch
body: Depending on the contract you have, data charges may apply for accessing the internet on your mobile device.If you are not sure about the potential charges, please ask your mobile network provider. You may find some costs are included in your existing price plan or that you can opt for a data package that gives reduced charges for accessing the internet.The BBC does not charge you to access mobile content.

schema.headline: The BBC News app keeps you informed with live and breaking news you can trust
